title: CI: format cirrus logs
body:
This introduces a new cirrus helper script, logformatter.
Usage is:

        [commands...] | logformatter TEST-NAME

It reformats its input into a readable, highlighed, linkable
form. Some features:

   - boring stuff (timestamps, standard podman options) is
     deemphasized
   - important stuff (warnings, errors) is emphasized
   - in-page links to the actual failures
   - active links to source files
   - jumps to bottom of page on load, because that's where
     the errors are. (All errors are linked)

Add it to select test commands (integration, system) and
add a new artifacts_html, run in the 'always' block, which
uploads generated *.log.html into Cirrus; from there we
generate a live URL that can be viewed in browser.

Unfortunately, due to security concerns in Cirrus, it is
not currently possible to make the link a live one.

Kludge: add a line of dashes after Restoring images; without this,
the first test ("systemd PID 1") has no dashes before it, so
logformatter doesn't see it.

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body:
@cevich PTAL; if you can provide hints on how to run this in the CI environment, and even better how to link to results from github, I'd welcome advice.

Possibilities for improvement:
* ~~Some tests fail three times; find some way to differentiate between those~~ solved!
* Find some way to link back to github PR and/or to Cirrus results, so page isn't a dead end
** SOLVED - partly (some logs include "cirrus-task-xyz"). Can we get it from the log file path?
* What do other people look for in ginkgo results, and how can we make that easier to read?

> Find some way to link back to github PR and/or to Cirrus results, so page isn't a dead end

There's [a slew of available env. vars.](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5970769217847296?command=setup_environment#L8-L33) that can be used to discover these things ([doc ref](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/writing-tasks/#environment-variables)).  Also important: Results may be from a human on local repo (`$CI != 'true'`), on a PR (`$CIRRUS_PR` not empty), on branch `$CIRRUS_BRANCH` (`$CIRRUS_PR` is empty), or because a tag was set (`$CIRRUS_PR` empty and `$CIRRUS_TAG` not empty).

> What do other people look for in ginkgo results, and how can we make that easier to read?

Brent said they like to see the verbose logs from successfully running tests, every now and again.

There's also a Ginkgo testing SNAFU: If one of the nodes (threads) hangs, the main output will be completely munged.  The only way to debug this is by examining one of the node-logs `$CIRRUS_WORKING_DIR/test/e2e/ginkgo-node-*.log`.  In fact, those files may be easier/cleaner for you to parse anyway, and are missing all the make/build output.

From whence ginkgo gets run:  https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/Makefile#L296-L304

One level up:
* here https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/contrib/cirrus/integration_test.sh#L29-L73
* and here: https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/contrib/cirrus/container_test.sh#L133-L141

Possible environments:
* CI = true
   * Fedora
   * Previous Fedora
   * Ubuntu
   * Ubuntu LTS
   * Inside podman, inside Fedora using `$IN_PODMAN_IMAGE` (currently `quay.io/libpod/in_podman:latest`)
* CI != true
   * Inside VM spawned by hack/get_ci_vm.sh (or some other means)
   * On a developers workstation
   * In a container on a developers workstation (possibly using any random container image)

>From whence ginkgo gets run

Unfortunately none of that is helpful, because none of that gives me the path to the log file. Basically, at some point in time the file `integration_test.log` is getting created; I need to run this script with a path to that file. My guess is that Cirrus does this automagically: for any given `foo_task` it creates a `foo.log`. I just need a way to run after that gets created, then create a new file.

I don't think we can rely on `/tmp/integration_test.log` as that's internal Cirrus-CI behavior, it could change at any time.  There's a background cirrus agent involved, and somehow streaming that content into a storage bucket.  We really have no control over that file, it's name, or when it's contents are valid.

If post-processing the logs is the only way (vs a pipe), we should rely on our own tooling to make that happen.  Best idea I have is adding a pipe through `tee /tmp/raw_intg_output.log` and then wire in post-processing via `contrib/cirrus/logcollector.sh`.  That's called from here: https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/.cirrus.yml#L406-L413

Note there's a way to directly attach generated files into the results as well (which means they'll have a public-facing URI).  For example: https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/.cirrus.yml#L398-L401 [Ref doc.](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/writing-tasks/#artifacts-instruction)

Artifact instruction example effect can be seen in the Cirrus-CI WebUI:

https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5689294241136640

That tarball is stored here:

https://storage.cloud.google.com/cirrus-ci-5385732420009984-fcae48/artifacts/containers/libpod/5689294241136640/tar/podman-release.tar.gz

So...if we know the artifact filename we can construct a direct url to it using during task execution by the format:

`https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-$SOME_MAGIC_NUMBER/$CIRRUS_TASK_ID'$ARTIFACT_NAME/$FILENAME`
Also note, google correctly set the `Content-Type=application/x-tar` for that file, so perhaps if your output ends in `.html` we can make this work.
Err, correction: We can set whatever mine-type we want when using the cirrus-ci `artifact` instruction.

Good news, bad news.

Good news: the filter seems to have worked, created artifacts, and [uploaded to Cirrus](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4770583556653056)

Bad news:
* It only saves the artifacts on success. The alert reader might realize the problem with this.
* They're not exactly in an obvious place. And
* Cirrus serves them with `Content-Disposition: attachment`, so there's no sane way to view in the browser.

Giving up for now.

The logs look great, even if slightly huge.  That's a HUGE bit of good-news.

> Cirrus serves them with Content-Disposition: attachment, so there's no sane way to view in the browser.

Wait, this isn't horrible actually, we aren't obligated to access the file through the Cirrus WebUI...They're using a google storage bucket we have full access to.  So the only challenge is forming and presenting the URL back to the developer.  For example one of the archives yours uploaded for:

```https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4770583556653056```

can be accessed as:

https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-5385732420009984-fcae48/artifacts/containers/libpod/4770583556653056/html/integration_test.log.html

The format for that from within any script being run by cirrus can be formed like:

```
URL_BASE="https://storage.googleapis.com"
STATIC_MAGIC_BLOB="cirrus-ci-5385732420009984-fcae48"
ARTIFACT_NAME="html"
LOGFILE_NAME="integration_test.log.html"

URL="${URL_BASE}/${STATIC_MAGIC_BLOB}/artifacts/${CIRRUS_REPO_FULL_NAME}/${CIRRUS_TASK_ID}/${ARTIFACT_NAME}/${LOGFILE_NAME}"
```

IIRC, the `STATIC_MAGIC_BLOB` is formed from some combination of an internal github ID and the google project number.  I/We could do more research to expose the magic better if needed.

So really, the next issues to consider are:

* How best get the URLs in-front of the developers eyeballs.
* How to presenting the URLs from various parts of the matrix, w/o driving developers insane.
* Make sure both the above also function when a task is re-run.
* Make sure all the above doesn't break horribly when running post-merge or post-tag (i.e. not in a PR).

Using the magic strings you provided above, I've added a link to the bottom of each log:
```
Annotated results: http://....
```
Cirrus won't make it a live link, so it's up to the reader to triple-click then paste in their browser.
fantastic, taking a peek...
Thanks @cevich. This is still WIP because I need to make the logger script much more robust.

> This is still WIP because I need to make the logger script much more robust.

If you like, just don't end up like me and include 5,000,000,000 commits into one PR :grin: 

(logging script _could_ be an incremental improvement, just sayin')

Sample log showing the ["timed out waiting for file"](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-5385732420009984-fcae48/artifacts/containers/libpod/5025576838758400/html/integration_test.log.html) flake
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #5333) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
/approve

Thought-food on future improvements:  

Background:

You may notice that the storage path is differentiated by the 'task ID' (big number).  Tasks can be re-run, and will be assigned a new ID at that time.  However, the overall build (higher level container of all tasks) keeps it's original ID, regardless of re-runs.  The 'build ID' only changes when an entirely new run is triggered (e.g. by a force-push or 're-run all').

From the POV of each task, they can access their higher-level `$CIRRUS_BUILD_ID` and (obviously) their own `$CIRRUS_TASK_ID`.  But there is no way (that I am aware of) for any task to access the entire collection of task ID's for their build.  This includes during, and after the build is complete.

Request:

Making the build ID -> task IDs association is critical for being able to generate summaries of your pretty logs, tabulate test-trends, or (somethig I'm interested in) track flakes and re-runs (esp. at the branch level).  If you can think of a simple way to build up a tasks-of-a-build association, I'm keenly interested in NOT doing it the complicated way I thought of :smile:
Do you mean the 'All other runs' table [here](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6390221092683776)? It could be scraped, I guess; I'm just not sure how useful that is

> Do you mean the 'All other runs' table

No, one level higher:  All the tasks from the perspective of a build.  Including tasks that were re-run.

For ref: my "complicated" idea was:  Add a script run by each task which simply posts it's own `$CIRRUS_TASK_ID` value as the name of a file into a storage bucket.  The path used in the bucket, would include the `$CIRRUS_BUILD_ID`.  Put simply, we'd end up (eventually) with a directory for each build, containing a reference for all associated task ID that ever ran or were re-run later.

Then we have a periodic job/script loop through all the bucket's build-id paths, find the "new" (def. TBD) builds, and process them.  Where "process" could be (among other things) generating an `index.html` summary table with links to all your annotated logs for that build.  Then it would post a link to the index table into the PR as a comment, or on IRC, or whatever.

It looks like the task list can be queried via the Cirrus [API](https://cirrus-ci.org/api/), but I'm completely unable to figure out how to actually use said API.

I'm also having trouble understanding the target audience for this: when I submit a PR, I just keep hitting Re-run until it passes, I don't feel a strong need to review past results. What do you see as the use case for this?

Yes, I've looked at their API, and even envisioned some ways it could be used.  The challenge is the "final" build is never really concluded, tasks could be re-run at any ime.  Though probably the likelihood of a re-run rapidly diminishes with time, or followup builds.

> I don't feel a strong need to review past results. What do you see as the use case for this?

All of them hinge around accessing test data on the large, at various time-scales.  Some ideas:

1. Help investigating emerging race-conditions, often obscured by people smashing re-run on active PRs.  For example, on the recent exec issue, quickly seeing if there are examples on F30 only or F31.
2. Tracking flakes across active PRs, branches, and releases.  Easily see if a specific test or environment is producing more flakes than another.  See if the number of task re-runs is trending up/down from one release to the next, or month-by-month, etc.
3. On an individual PR, see if a certain test is failing across multiple environments at a glance.  Are all the failed tests common across every environment or only some of them.  
4. On an individual PR, if there are only a few failures (<=5 for example), provide a list of direct links in a followup comment, providing quick access with minimal clicks. (requires rapid post-processing)
5. Track individual test performance trends over time, per environment.  Compare test performance from a PR to the current average for the branch.
6. During runtime, allow accessing a VM from another task (VMs are named with their associated task number).  For example, non-local testing of podman-remote (or APIv2).  Test using NFS/CIFS mounts in containers or as container storage.

This is something that of course would be much better hyperlinked, but that won't happen with Cirrus.

Oh cool!  I wanna see how you did it...but first:

> that won't happen with Cirrus.

Correction: with**in** Cirrus.

We don't _have_ to use their WebUI for all things.  Another easily accessible option (which also supports GraphQL) is the Github API.  A script could simply post either a link or the summarized build results directly into the PR as a comment.  If the summary is potentially large, it could be attached and linked in a comment [like_this.txt](https://github.com/containers/libpod/files/4287910/like_this.txt)

For branches/tags, it's possible to post comments directly to a commit SHA, for example: https://github.com/containers/libpod/commit/d5f5b2e8becfb3a6e13d8b3248e26f3fbe22bdb4

awww...script not here :(

I have an idea I want to try for possibly a simple way to run something like that, guaranteed to trigger upon completion of all tasks: #5392

>awww...script not here :(

Yeah - there is _no way_ I am updating this PR now that it finally passed CI.

I like your idea and will work toward getting a script usable in that context.

> Yeah - there is no way I am updating this PR now that it finally passed CI.

Lol, ya I understand :grin: 

> I like your idea and will work toward getting a script usable in that context

Thanks.  I've got some other ideas on how to trigger it on completion of a build in case my test PR doesn't work out.  Then worst-case, Cirrus-CI can be configured to fire off `POST` request to an arbitrary REST API of our choosing, that includes all the build/task details in it's JSON payload.
Can we merge this then?
Yes please. It might need small tweaks but I think it's working as expected.
/approve
/lgtm

I tweaked my cirrus-flake-summarize script (separate entity from logformatter) to produce github markup suitable for a comment. In combination with #5392 it could be used to alert QE to recurring flakes.
